check: Authorization checked on every request, not inferred from UI state
description: Every endpoint verifies the authenticated principal may act on the specific resource requested, server-side, regardless of what the UI shows or hides
why_it_matters: Hiding a button in the UI is not access control — an attacker can call the endpoint directly; this is consistently the most common real-world vulnerability class

check: No insecure direct object references (IDOR)
description: Resource IDs in URLs/params are checked for ownership before returning data, not just checked for existence (e.g. /orders/123 must verify the caller owns order 123, not just that order 123 exists)
why_it_matters: IDOR lets an attacker enumerate sequential/guessable IDs to access other users' data by simply changing a URL parameter

check: Default deny, not default allow
description: New endpoints require explicit authorization logic to be added; the default behavior for an unclassified route is to deny, not to serve
why_it_matters: A forgotten auth check on a new endpoint should fail closed, not open — this inverts the risk of the single most common access-control mistake

check: CORS configured to an explicit allowlist, never a wildcard on authenticated routes
description: Access-Control-Allow-Origin is set to specific known origins for any endpoint that reads/writes authenticated data — never * combined with credentials
why_it_matters: A wildcard CORS policy on an authenticated endpoint lets any website make credentialed requests on behalf of a logged-in user

check: No sensitive data transmitted or stored unencrypted
description: PII, credentials, and financial data are encrypted in transit (TLS everywhere, no plain HTTP) and at rest (database-level or column-level encryption for the most sensitive fields)
why_it_matters: Cryptographic failures are the direct cause of many major breaches becoming catastrophic instead of contained — encryption is what limits blast radius when other defenses fail

check: Passwords hashed with a modern memory-hard algorithm (Argon2id), never reversibly encrypted
description: Password storage uses Argon2id (or bcrypt as an acceptable fallback) — never a fast general-purpose hash (MD5/SHA family) and never reversible encryption
why_it_matters: A password should never be recoverable in plaintext by anyone, including the system operator — hashing (not encryption) with a memory-hard algorithm is the only acceptable approach

check: No hardcoded cryptographic keys or secrets in source code
description: Encryption keys, signing secrets, and API credentials are loaded from environment variables or a secrets manager, never committed to the repository
why_it_matters: A hardcoded key in source is compromised the moment the repo is cloned, forked, or leaked — and is the single most common finding in automated secret-scanning tools

check: TLS 1.2+ enforced, weak cipher suites disabled
description: The server/platform rejects TLS 1.0/1.1 connections and disables known-weak cipher suites — usually a platform-level default to verify rather than a manual configuration on managed hosting
why_it_matters: Older TLS versions and weak ciphers have known cryptographic weaknesses that make traffic interception or downgrade attacks feasible

check: All database queries parameterized, never string-concatenated
description: Every SQL/NoSQL query uses parameterized queries or an ORM's query builder — raw string concatenation of user input into a query is never used
why_it_matters: SQL injection remains a critical, fully preventable vulnerability class — parameterized queries eliminate the entire attack surface by construction, not by filtering

check: User input never passed to a shell command, eval, or dynamic code execution
description: Shell commands, eval(), exec(), and similar dynamic-execution constructs never incorporate unsanitized user input; prefer a library call over shelling out entirely where possible
why_it_matters: Command injection via string-built shell commands is a critical, often fully exploitable vulnerability — the safest mitigation is avoiding the pattern entirely, not trying to sanitize it safely

check: Output encoded/escaped for its rendering context (HTML, JS, URL, SQL)
description: Any user-supplied data rendered into HTML, injected into a script context, or embedded in a URL is encoded appropriately for that specific context, not encoded once generically
why_it_matters: Context matters — HTML-escaping alone doesn't prevent injection into a JavaScript string or a URL parameter; each output context has its own encoding requirement

check: Framework's built-in template auto-escaping left enabled
description: React/Vue/Django templates/etc. auto-escape interpolated values by default — this is never disabled (e.g. dangerouslySetInnerHTML, |safe filters) without a specific, reviewed reason and explicit sanitization
why_it_matters: Auto-escaping is the default defense against XSS in modern frameworks; disabling it removes that defense for exactly the data that most needs it

check: Threat modeling performed for features handling sensitive data or money
description: New features touching auth, payments, or PII go through an explicit threat-modeling pass before implementation, not just after a vulnerability is found in review
why_it_matters: Insecure design vulnerabilities can't be patched the way a code-level bug can — they require rethinking the feature, which is far cheaper to do before implementation than after launch

check: Rate limiting and abuse controls designed in from the start for high-value actions
description: Signup, login, password reset, and any action with real-world cost (sending SMS/email, processing a payment) have rate limits designed as part of the feature, not bolted on after an abuse incident
why_it_matters: Retrofitting rate limiting after an abuse incident is reactive and slower than designing the constraint in from the start — high-value actions are predictable abuse targets

check: No default credentials or example configuration left in production
description: Default admin passwords, example API keys, and sample configuration from a framework's scaffolding are removed or changed before production deployment
why_it_matters: Default credentials are one of the most trivially exploitable misconfigurations — automated scanners specifically probe for known framework defaults

check: Verbose error messages and stack traces disabled in production
description: Production error responses return a generic message and a correlation id (see BE069), never a stack trace, file path, or internal implementation detail
why_it_matters: Verbose errors leak implementation details (library versions, file structure, query fragments) that materially help an attacker plan further exploitation

check: Unnecessary features, ports, and services disabled
description: Debug endpoints, admin panels not meant for public access, and unused services are disabled or firewalled off in production, not left reachable 'just in case'
why_it_matters: Every enabled feature/service is additional attack surface — anything not actively needed in production should not be reachable from it

check: Security headers set on every response (CSP, HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options)
description: Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, and X-Frame-Options/frame-ancestors are set via middleware on every response, not per-route ad hoc
why_it_matters: These headers are cheap, broad-coverage defenses against XSS, clickjacking, MIME-sniffing attacks, and protocol downgrade — missing them is a common, easily-fixed finding

check: Dependencies scanned for known vulnerabilities in CI
description: An automated dependency-vulnerability scan (npm audit, pip-audit, Dependabot/Snyk alerts) runs on every PR or on a schedule, not just when someone remembers to check manually
why_it_matters: Known-vulnerable dependencies are one of the most common breach vectors precisely because the fix (upgrade) is usually available but simply not applied promptly

check: Dependencies kept reasonably current, not years out of date
description: Automated dependency-update PRs (DO017) are actually merged on a regular cadence, not left accumulating indefinitely
why_it_matters: A vulnerability scanner finding a known CVE is only useful if the resulting update actually gets applied — an ignored Dependabot backlog provides no real protection

check: No credential stuffing/brute-force vulnerability on login
description: Login endpoints have rate limiting and/or progressive delays by account and by IP (DB040), and ideally CAPTCHA or similar after repeated failures
why_it_matters: Automated credential-stuffing attacks specifically target unprotected login endpoints at scale using leaked password lists from other breaches

check: Session tokens invalidated on logout and on password change
description: Logging out actually invalidates the session/token server-side (not just clearing it client-side), and a password change invalidates all other active sessions (DB039)
why_it_matters: A session that remains valid server-side after logout or password change means the 'security action' the user took didn't actually close the exposure it was meant to close

check: MFA available for accounts, required for privileged/admin accounts
description: Multi-factor authentication is offered to all users and required, not optional, for admin/high-privilege accounts specifically
why_it_matters: Admin accounts are the highest-value target for credential-based attacks — MFA there is the single highest-leverage auth control available

check: CI/CD pipeline verifies artifact integrity before deploy
description: Build artifacts are verified (checksums, signed commits, or a trusted build pipeline with no manual intervention) before being deployed to production
why_it_matters: An attacker who can tamper with the build/deploy pipeline can inject malicious code without touching the reviewed source — pipeline integrity is as important as code review

check: Deserialization of untrusted data avoided or strictly validated
description: Untrusted data is never deserialized into native objects/classes without strict schema validation — prefer plain data formats (JSON with schema validation) over language-native serialization for anything crossing a trust boundary
why_it_matters: Insecure deserialization of untrusted data can lead directly to remote code execution in several languages' native serialization formats

check: Security-relevant events logged (auth failures, access-control failures, high-value actions)
description: Failed login attempts, authorization failures, password changes, and admin actions are logged with enough context (who, what, when, from where) to investigate an incident after the fact
why_it_matters: Without security event logging, detecting an ongoing attack or investigating a suspected breach after the fact is far slower or entirely impossible

check: Logs don't contain sensitive data (passwords, full card numbers, tokens)
description: Log statements are reviewed/redact sensitive fields before writing — a password or full credit card number should never appear in a log line, even at debug level
why_it_matters: Logs are often less tightly access-controlled than the primary database, and are frequently retained longer — sensitive data there is a second, often-overlooked exposure surface

check: Alerting configured on anomalous security events, not just infrastructure metrics
description: A spike in failed logins, a burst of 403s, or repeated access-control failures from one source triggers an alert, not just uptime/latency monitoring
why_it_matters: Infrastructure monitoring alone won't catch a credential-stuffing attack or an authorization-bypass attempt in progress — that requires security-event-specific alerting

check: Server-side requests to user-supplied URLs are validated against an allowlist
description: Any feature where the server fetches a URL provided by the user (webhook registration, URL preview, image fetch by URL) validates the destination against an allowlist and blocks internal/private IP ranges
why_it_matters: Server-Side Request Forgery lets an attacker use your server as a proxy to reach internal infrastructure (cloud metadata endpoints, internal services) that wouldn't otherwise be reachable from outside

check: Cloud metadata endpoint access blocked from application code paths
description: Requests to the cloud provider's internal metadata endpoint (a well-known link-local address) are blocked at the network level for any process that fetches user-influenced URLs
why_it_matters: The cloud metadata endpoint frequently holds IAM credentials — SSRF-to-metadata-endpoint is one of the most damaging real-world SSRF exploitation patterns

check: Input validated at every trust boundary, not just the first one
description: Validation happens at each system boundary where untrusted data enters (API request, message queue consumer, webhook receiver, file upload) — not assumed to have already been validated upstream
why_it_matters: A validation check performed once at the edge doesn't protect an internal service that's reachable through another path (a queue consumer, an internal API) — every entry point is its own trust boundary

check: File uploads validated by content, restricted by type and size, stored outside the web root
description: Uploaded files are validated by actual content (not just the claimed extension/MIME type), size-limited, and stored somewhere that can't be directly executed if uploaded content turns out to be malicious
why_it_matters: An unrestricted file upload is a common path to remote code execution (uploading an executable script disguised as an image) or storage abuse (unbounded uploads)

check: Admin/internal tools require stronger authentication than customer-facing surfaces
description: Internal admin panels and support tools require MFA and/or are only reachable from a VPN/allowlisted network, not just the same login as customer accounts
why_it_matters: Admin tooling has disproportionately high blast radius if compromised (access to all customer data, ability to impersonate users) and deserves a correspondingly higher authentication bar

check: Third-party integrations reviewed for the data access they're granted
description: Every third-party SDK/service integrated (analytics, chat widgets, payment processors) is reviewed for what data it can access and scoped to the minimum needed
why_it_matters: Each third-party integration is effectively code execution or data access granted to an external party — unreviewed integrations are a common, underappreciated source of data exposure

check: Regular dependency and configuration review, not just at launch
description: Security review of dependencies, configuration, and access grants happens on a recurring cadence (quarterly or similar), not only during initial launch hardening
why_it_matters: Security posture decays over time as new dependencies are added, permissions accumulate, and new attack techniques emerge — a one-time launch review doesn't stay valid indefinitely
